coredata.dc:title: Extremely low loading of carbon quantum dots for high energy density in polyetherimide nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Haoran
Luo, Hang
Liu, Yuan
Guo, Ru
Ji, Xiaobo
Hou, Hongshuai
Zhang, Dou
coredata.dc:description: There is an urgent need for dielectric capacitors with high energy density and efficiency with the rapid progress of power electronic devices. Inorganic/organic composites were once considered as a potential candidate for dielectrics. However, the inevitable defects induced by the inorganic fillers always lead to the decreased breakdown strength and limited energy density and efficiency. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with abundant functional groups were synthetized and introduced into linear dielectric polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix. Due to the unique Coulomb-blockade effect of quantum dots, the movement of carriers can be hindered under the applied electric field, leading to the improvement of breakdown strength. Compared with most of the reported nanocomposites with high loading of fillers, the nanocomposites in this work with extremely low loading of CQDs exhibit obviously enhanced performance. The nanocomposite with 0.5 wt% CQDs exhibits a high discharge energy density of 10.66 J/cm3 and efficiency of 88.3% at 600 kV/mm, corresponding to a 62.3% improvement over that of PEI (6.57 J/cm3 at 490 kV/mm). This work provides an effective strategy to break down the long-standing contradiction between the enhancement of dielectric constant and the reduced in breakdown strength. The film dielectrics with extremely low loading of fillers contributing to enhanced energy density and high energy efficiency is very important for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dielectric capacitors
Carbon quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine
Energy density

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design and schematic manufacturing process of the CQDs/PEI composites.
(a), (b), (c) TEM images of CQDs under different magnifications, (d) photographs of CQDs powder and solution in NMP solvent.
(a) The survey and (b) C1s XPS spectrum, (c) FTIR spectrum, (d) XRD pattern, (e) UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy and (f) (αhv)2-hv plot of CQDs.
Cross-sectional SEM image of (a) pure PEI and composites with different CQDs content: (b) 0.05 wt%, (c) 0.2 wt%, (d) 0.5 wt%, (e) 1.0 wt%. (f) DSC curve of pure PEI and composites.
Frequency-dependent (a) dielectric constant, (b) dielectric loss, temperature-dependent (c) dielectric constant and (d) dielectric loss of pure PEI and composites.
(a) Weibull distribution of the breakdown strength, (b) characteristic breakdown strength, (c) leakage current density, (d) load–displacement curve and Young’s modulus of pure PEI and composites.
Displacement hysteresis loops of (a) pure PEI and composites with different CQDs content: (b) 0.05 wt%, (c) 0.2 wt%, (d) 0.5 wt%, (e) 1.0 wt%. (f) Pm-Pr of pure PEI and composites.

(a) Discharge energy density and efficiency, (b) charge–discharge cycle performance of pure PEI and composites. (c) The energy storage performance tested at different regions (as marked in the illustration) of the large CQDs/PEI film. The average values and max–min error bars of the results were obtained from five parallel samples. (d) Overdamped discharge curves and (e) time dependence of Wd under different electric fields of the composites with 0.5 wt% CQDs. (f) Time dependence of Wd under 300 kV/mm of commerical BOPP and the composites with 0.5 wt% CQDs.
Comparison of energy efficiency and discharge energy density in some reported advanced polymer-based composites.[23,25,34,35,37,52–58]

head.ce:title: Extremely low loading of carbon quantum dots for high energy density in polyetherimide nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an urgent need for dielectric capacitors with high energy density and efficiency with the rapid progress of power electronic devices. Inorganic/organic composites were once considered as a potential candidate for dielectrics. However, the inevitable defects induced by the inorganic fillers always lead to the decreased breakdown strength and limited energy density and efficiency. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with abundant functional groups were synthetized and introduced into linear dielectric polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix. Due to the unique Coulomb-blockade effect of quantum dots, the movement of carriers can be hindered under the applied electric field, leading to the improvement of breakdown strength. Compared with most of the reported nanocomposites with high loading of fillers, the nanocomposites in this work with extremely low loading of CQDs exhibit obviously enhanced performance. The nanocomposite with 0.5 wt% CQDs exhibits a high discharge energy density of 10.66 J/cm3 and efficiency of 88.3% at 600 kV/mm, corresponding to a 62.3% improvement over that of PEI (6.57 J/cm3 at 490 kV/mm). This work provides an effective strategy to break down the long-standing contradiction between the enhancement of dielectric constant and the reduced in breakdown strength. The film dielectrics with extremely low loading of fillers contributing to enhanced energy density and high energy efficiency is very important for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Dielectric capacitors have broad application prospects in pulse power equipment, hybrid vehicles, and 5G communications owing to their ultra-high power density.[1–7] Currently, the most widely used dielectric material in commercial film capacitors is biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP),[8,9] which has a high breakdown strength of 640 kV/mm.[10] However, due to the limitation of low dielectric constant (∼2.2), the energy storage density of BOPP is only 1 ∼ 2 J/cm3.[11] In practical applications, low energy storage density means large volume and quantity of capacitor devices, which seriously limits the development of electronic equipment.[12,13] Therefore, it is urgent to increase the energy storage density of dielectric capacitors.

The energy storage density (Ue ) of dielectrics is expressed as
U e
= ∫ E d D = ∫
ε 0
ε r
E d E
, where εr , ε0 and E are the dielectric constant, the vacuum dielectric constant and the applied electric field, respectively.[14,15] Obviously, high Ue is based on high εr and breakdown strength (Eb ). A large number of studies have demonstrated that preparing polymer-based composites by combining the advantages of high Eb from polymer matrix with high εr from ceramic nanofillers is a common strategy.[16–23] After the ceramic nanofillers with high loading were introduced into the polymer, the dielectric constant of the composites was obviously increased, not only due to the high dielectric constant of nanofillers themselves, but also the contribution of interface polarization.[24–28] However, this strategy cannot avoid the decrease in breakdown strength and the improvement of the energy density is still limited due to the following reasons.[20,29] Firstly, there is a large difference in surface properties between the filler and matrix because the surface of ceramic filler usually lacks of functional groups, and therefore, the formation of tight bonding with the polymer matrix is difficult, resulting in agglomeration and defects in the composites.[30] Secondly, the phenomenon of local electric field concentration usually arises in the composites due to the orders of magnitude difference in the dielectric constant between the polymer matrix (usually < 10) and the ceramic filler, i.e. greater than 1000.[30–33]

Recently, some researches proved that introducing well-dispersed quantum dot fillers into the composites could suppress the leakage current density and improve breakdown strength due to the Coulomb-blockade effect.[34–37] Quantum dots are nanoconductors or semiconductor nanoparticles with sizes ranging from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers and have obvious quantum effects.[38] Uncharged quantum dots have an obvious trapping effect on electrons, and quantum dots with an electron can build a potential barrier of e2/2C in the insulating system.[35] If the barrier is much larger than the thermal energy of the electron, it will prevent the second electron from passing through, that is called Coulomb-blockade effect.[35,39] It means that the quantum dots uniformly dispersed in the polymer matrix can delay the movement of carriers, which will reduce the probability of electrical breakdown and enhance the insulation performance of the composites.[40] This provides a new strategy for solving the contradiction between increasing the breakdown strength and increasing the dielectric constant. Yang et al. developed a novel strawberry-like nanoparticles by using nano-Ag modified BaTiO3 nanoparticles coated with polydopamine (PDA) and added it to the P(VDF-HFP) matrix.[36] By uniformly modifying the nano-Ag on the PDA shell, the Coulomb-blockade effect is successfully introduced into the composites. As a result, composites exhibited excellent electric properties such as suppressed conduction loss, improved breakdown strength, energy density and energy efficiency. Xing et al. reported Ag/PI dielectric composites prepared by the in situ polymerization process.[37] Owing to the Coulomb-blockade effect, the energy storage performance of the composites was significantly improved by loading low-content Ag nanodots. In particular, the nanocomposite film with 0.1 vol% Ag can reach an energy density of 5.16 J/cm3 at 600 kV/mm, while maintaining an efficiency of 67.7%. However, metal nanodots, such as Ag quantum dots lack functional groups on the surface. Even if the secondary surface modification is performed, their dispersibility and compatibility in the polymer matrix are still limited, and therefore, the advantages of the Coulomb-blockade effect cannot be fully utilized.[34]

In this work, CQDs with diameters of 1.0–4.0 nm were prepared and composited with polyetherimide (PEI) via a solution-casting method. The CQDs presented monodispersity in the solution and polymer matrix due to the abundant surface functional groups such as C = O and –OH,[41] and the groups on the surface of CQDs can form a tight bond with the matrix. The especial Coulomb-blockade effect from CQDs brought enhanced breakdown strength due to the scattering center CQDs bended the propagation path of carriers in the composites. At the same time, the small-sized CQDs caused the enhancement of the interface polarization, resulting in an increase in the dielectric constant of the composites. Notably, the breakdown strength and dielectric constant of the CQDs/PEI composites with a low content of CQDs were synergistically enhanced. The composite with 0.5 wt% CQDs showed an excellent discharge energy density of 10.66 J/cm3 at 600 kV/mm, and maintain a high efficiency of 88.3%. This work proposed an achievable strategy to break down the inversion relationship between dielectric constant and breakdown strength and provided an effective route to prepare dielectric capacitors with high discharge energy density and efficiency.

NaOH, acetaldehyde solution (40% in H2O) and HCl solution (1 M) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. PEI pellets and commercial BOPP films were provided by PolyK Technologies, LLC, USA. 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) solvent was supplied by Sinopharm, China and used without further purification.

300 g NaOH and 1000 mL acetaldehyde solution were mixed and stirred for 2 h. Next, dilute HCl solution was added until the pH was adjusted to neutral. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with deionized water. Finally, approximately 150 g CQDs powder was obtained after drying at 100 ℃ for 12 h.

CQDs/PEI composites were fabricated by solution-casting method. CQDs were dispersed in NMP by ultrasound for 10 min, then PEI pellets were added and stirred at 70 ℃ for 12 h. Finally, the solution was cast on a glass plate and dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h. The schematic diagram of the design and manufacturing process of the composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan), scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM230, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250xi, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/max 2550, Japan), fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR, Is-50, USA) and UV–vis spectrometer (TU-1901, China) were used to characterize the microscopic morphology, structure and composition of the samples. The differential scanning calorimetry analyzer (DSC, DSC-25, American) was employed to measure the DSC curve in a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 ℃/min. The ultra nanoindenter (UNHT, Switzerland) was used to test the load–displacement curve of the samples. The electrodes with an area of 3.14 mm2 were sputtered on both surfaces of the sample for electrical performance testing. The dielectric properties were characterized using an Agilent 4294A LCR meter from 1 kHz to 10 MHz. The high temperature dielectric properties were carried out through a high temperature dielectric temperature spectrum measurement system (DMS-500, China) at 1 kHz from 30 ℃ to 200 ℃. The displacement hysteresis loops were characterized using a TF Analyzer 2000 series (aixACT, Germany) at 10 Hz. The pulse discharge performance was tested by dielectric material charge measurement system (DCQ-20A, PolyK Technologies, USA).

Fig. 2 a-c show the TEM images of the CQDs powders with diameters of 1.0–4.0 nm under different magnifications. It can be found that the CQDs present a distinct monodisperse state. Fig. 2d displays the photos of yellow CQDs powders and solution in NMP solvent. Obviously, CQDs can be well dispersed in NMP solvent, and the resulting solution is uniform and transparent. XPS (Fig. 3 a, b) and FTIR (Fig. 3c) show that CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, such as C = O and –OH, which facilitates its good dispersion and interfacial compatibility in the polymer matrix. The XRD results of CQDs (Fig. 3d) show a broad peak around 20°, demonstrating a highly amorphous structure. According to the calculation of UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy (Fig. 3e), the curve of (αhv)2-hv (Fig. 3f) can be obtained, where α, h, and v are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant and light frequency, respectively.[42] The band gap (Eg ) of CQDs can be determined to be about 2.16 eV through the start of absorption in the linear region, which proves it is a typical semiconductor.

Fig. 4 a-e show the cross-sectional micro-morphology images of pure PEI and composites. It can be observed that there are no obvious defects in the films with different contents of CQDs, even if the contents reached 1.0 wt%. The results show that CQDs have good compatibility with the PEI matrix, which can avoid the decrease in dielectric and mechanical properties caused by the introduction of fillers.[30] Fig. 4f shows the second heating DSC curve of the samples, and the curves present the same trend of change. The glass transition temperature (Tg ) of all composites is higher than 150 ℃, which is close to that of pure PEI films. The results show that the introduction of CQDs has little effect on the thermal stability of the PEI matrix.

The frequency-dependent dielectric constant and loss of the composites are displayed in Fig. 5 a and b. The dielectric constant increases with increasing content of CQDs, which can be attributed to the interface polarization caused by the increase of the interface regions. Therefore, the composite film loaded with 1.0 wt% CQDs exhibits a highest dielectric constant of 4.63, which is 8.4% higher than that of pure PEI (4.27). Unlike the change of dielectric constant, the dielectric loss exhibits a minimum value when the CQDs content is 0.5 wt%. Meanwhile, the dielectric loss of all the composites is lower than that of pure PEI, which is mainly due to the Coulomb-blockade effect. CQDs that have not captured electrons can act as traps in the electron transport path, while charged CQDs can act as an energy barrier for electron transport, ultimately making it difficult for electrons to move in the PEI matrix, resulting in reduced conduction loss.[37] When the filler content is low, the Coulomb-blockade effect increases as the filler content increases, which shows a stronger impact on the dielectric loss than the interface polarization. However, when the filler content exceeds 1.0 wt%, the reduction of the distance between CQDs makes electron conduction easier and the inhibitory effect on electron migration is weakened, resulting in an increase in conduction loss.[35,37,42] Therefore, the dielectric loss of the sample with 1.0 wt% CQDs is higher than that of the sample with 0.5 wt% CQDs. In particular, the dielectric loss of all composites is lower than 0.031 due to the rigid backbone of PEI.[43] Fig. 5c and d present the temperature-dependent dielectric properties of samples at 1 k Hz from 30 ℃ to 200 ℃. The results show that the dielectric properties of the sample remain stable up to about 140 ℃, which is consistent with the DSC test results. In particular, the dielectric loss of the CQDs/PEI composites maintains about 0.020 at 140 °C, which is beneficial for the practical application of capacitors at high temperatures.

The breakdown strength is an important parameter of dielectrics that determines energy storage density, and it can be calculated using the Weibull distribution function:
(1)
P
E
= 1 - e x p
( -
E /
E b
β
)

where P(E) is the cumulative probability of electric failure, E is the breakdown strength obtained through experimental tests, the characteristic breakdown strength Eb refers to the field strength when the breakdown probability is 63.2%, and the shape parameter β is used to evaluates the dispersion of data.[44,45] The fitting results and Eb value are presented in Fig. 6 a and b. The Eb value of pure PEI and composites with 0.05 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1.0 wt% CQDs are 467 kV/mm, 530 kV/mm, 544 kV/mm, 596 kV/mm, and 496 kV/mm, respectively. As the CQDs content increases, the breakdown strength of the composites increases significantly, reaching to a maximum value of 596 kV/mm at 0.5 wt% and then begins to decrease. In particular, the breakdown strength of all composites is higher than that of pure PEI (467 kV/mm).

Considering that the suppression of carrier migration caused by the Coulomb-blockade effect of CQDs can hinder the propagation of electrical trees and prevent composites from being broken down,[44] the leakage current density of pure PEI and composites from 0 to 200 kV/mm was tested to explore the reasons for the increase in breakdown strength, and the results are shown in Fig. 6c. The leakage current density of all the samples increases with the increase of the applied electric field and exhibits a nonlinear relationship. When the filler content is low, CQDs hinder the movement of carriers, resulting in the decrease of leakage current density. However, the restriction on the movement of carriers in the polymer matrix will be weakened with further increase of CQDs content, and a conductive path will be formed in the polymer matrix, leading to an increase of leakage current density.[46] It can be clearly seen that the relationship between leakage current density and filler content is consistent with the discussion about dielectric loss and breakdown strength. The 0.5 wt% CQDs/PEI composite shows the lowest leakage current density of 0.014 μA/cm2 under an electric field of 200 kV/mm,.

In addition to electrical breakdown, electromechanical failure is another breakdown mechanisms of polymers, which means that the breakdown strength of the material is closely related to the mechanical strength.[47,48] When the field-induced stress exceeds the yield stress of the polymers, it will cause the collapse of the electrodes and electrical discharging, which will lead to the breakdown of the polymers.[49] Therefore, the load–displacement curve of the composites was tested and the results of Young's modulus are presented in Fig. 6d. As the filler content increases, the Young's modulus of the composites increases significantly, and finally increases by 15% compared to pure PEI. The increased Young's modulus means a higher yield stress and the critical field of electromechanical failure, making the composites more difficult to be broken down.[49]

Another important parameter related to energy storage density is polarization (Pm -Pr ), where Pm and Pr are the maximum and remnant polarization.[37] The displacement hysteresis loops of the composites with different CQDs contents are shown in Fig. 7 a-e. The displacement hysteresis loops of all the samples exhibit similar linear characteristics. In addition, the Pm -Pr values of samples are illustrated in Fig. 7f. With the increase of filler content, the Pm -Pr values under the same electric field increases significantly, which can be attributed to the increase of Pm caused by the increased interface polarization. It can be seen that the composites with CQDs content of 0.5 wt% and 1.0 wt% under the same electric field possess much higher Pm -Pr than other samples. However, the composite with 0.5 wt% CQDs has the highest breakdown strength, reaching 3.66 μC/cm2 at 600 kV/mm, which is much higher than the 2.73 μC/cm2 of pure PEI.

The discharge energy density (Ue ) and efficiency (ƞ) are calculated by ƞ = Ue /U based on the displacement hysteresis loops, where U are the stored energy density,[50] and the results are presented in Fig. 8 a. The sample with 0.5 wt% CQDs exhibited the highest Ue of 10.66 J/cm3 at 600 kV/mm due to the highest breakdown strength and polarization, which is significantly higher than that of 6.57 J/cm3 at 490 kV/mm for the pure PEI. It is worth noting that the ƞ of the composites with 0.5 wt% CQDs can still remain to 88.3% even under the highest applied electric field of 600 kV/mm, and all composites still maintain a high ƞ of more than 90% under 500 kV/mm. In addition, the results of the Ue and ƞ under the conditions of 200 kV/mm and 1 kHz after 106 times charge–discharge cycles are shown in Fig. 8b. There is almost no decrease in the performance of the sample during the cyclic test, which indicates that the samples have excellent cycle performance. Considering the practical applications of dielectric capacitors, the uniformity of the performance of the large CQDs/PEI composites film (Fig. 8c) are also evaluated in order to guarantee the overall quality of the dielectric film. The energy storage density and efficiency at different regions of the large composites film with 0.5 wt% CQDs were measured, and the results exhibited a high degree of consistency in different film areas.

In order to obtain practical pulse discharge energy density, a load resistor of 100 kΩ was used to establish an overdamped state circuit. The overdamped discharge curves of the composites with 0.5 wt% CQDs in the electric field range of 50 to 300 kV/mm is shown in Fig. 8d, and a peak current of 0.03 A was qucikly obtained within a duration of about 0.2 μs. Pulse discharge energy density (Wd ) can be calculated by
W d
= R ∫
I
2
t
d t / V
,where R, I, t and V are load resistance, current, time and sampling volume, respectively.[51] The calculated curve is shown in Fig. 8e, a high Wd of 2.19 J/cm3 was achieved at 300 kV/mm and in a very rapid discharge duration of 10 μs, which exceeds that of commercial BOPP film (1.27 J/cm3).

Fig. 9 compares the energy storage performance of the 0.5 wt% CQDs/PEI composite with some polymer-based composites reported in the literature.[23,25,34,35,37,52–58] The most significant advantage of this work is that the energy efficiency is much higher than that of ferroelectric polymer-based composites, and the energy storage density is higher than that of linear polymer-based composites, since the 0.5 wt% CQDs/PEI composite can achieve a synergistic increase in dielectric constant and breakdown strength while maintaining extremely high energy efficiency.

In summary, we fabricated CQDs/PEI composite films through a typical solution casting method. The introduction of CQDs can increase the interface polarization and Young's modulus of composites. In addition, it can effectively inhibit the movement of carriers due to the Coulomb-blockade effect. Therefore, the dielectric constant and breakdown strength of the composites have been improved simultaneously. As a result, the 0.5 wt% CQDs composite exhibits a high efficiency of 88.3% and an excellent energy storage density of 10.66 J cm3 at 600 kV/mm, which is much higher than pure PEI (6.57 J/cm3 at 490 kV/mm). The excellent energy storage performances, especially high efficiency, greatly promote the practical application of these composites in advanced energy-storage devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support by National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2020YFA0711700), National Natural Science Foundation of China (U19A2087 and 52002404) and State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China.
